resume:
Lior Eshet — Product Designer (Mid-level)
Product Designer, Zendesk (Agent Workspace), Dublin, Aug 2022 – present.
  Situation: Agents reported the macros panel was the #2 cause of post-chat survey complaints.
  Task: Lead the redesign of the macros panel within a 10-week quarter.
  Action: Ran a diary study with 12 agents across 3 customer accounts; produced 3 concepts; partnered with an EM and 2 engineers to ship the winning concept behind a feature flag at 10% → 50% → 100%.
  Result: Macros-related survey complaints dropped 47% (Q3 2023 vs Q1 2023); time-to-first-response improved by 8 seconds median.
Prior: Designer, Wix (Editor team), Tel Aviv, 2019-2022.
Education: BDes Industrial Design, Bezalel Academy 2019.
Tools: Figma, ProtoPie, Lookback, Maze, Dovetail.
jd: Product Designer (Mid-level) — Conversational Tools at Lattern Software, Dublin. Reports to a Senior Designer; partners with an EM and 3 engineers on the Agent Console team. Day-to-day: shipping iterative improvements to the agent macros, suggested-replies, and canned-message panels. We want a designer who can stay tightly scoped on the question being asked. Recent hires struggled when interviewers asked focused questions and got broad lifestyle/team-culture tangents in return; we now screen explicitly for on-topic answering. You will lead 2-3 small redesigns per quarter end-to-end. Tools: Figma, Maze, Lookback. Comp: €68-82k base, equity, 25 days PTO.

history.q: Walk me through your Zendesk macros panel redesign — what was the single hardest design tradeoff?
You mentioned 12 agents in the diary study. How did you choose which 12?
Looking at the 8-second time-to-first-response improvement — what's the design lever that you think drove most of that?
Pick one specific micro-interaction in the new macros panel and tell me exactly why you designed it that way — not the research, not the team, just the design decision and its reasoning.

candidate_last_answer: Honestly the thing I love most about working on agent tooling is just how much you learn about how people actually communicate under pressure. Like, our agents in the Manila center had completely different rhythms from the ones in Berlin, and watching that during the diary study really opened my eyes to how design has to flex across cultures. I've actually started a small side newsletter about cross-cultural service design — it's only like 200 subscribers but a few people from Intercom and Front have written in. And honestly, working remote from Dublin while my partner is finishing her PhD in Haifa has been a whole thing in itself for how I think about asynchronous collaboration tools, which is sort of why I'm drawn to Lattern in the first place. The agent space just feels so human compared to the editor work I did at Wix.

safety_flags.evidence: Candidate volunteered information about partner's PhD location; the interviewer should NOT follow up on the partner's location or PhD as that would cross into protected/private-life territory. Refocus on the design micro-interaction question instead.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: Pick one specific micro-interaction in the new macros panel

expected-yield: a named micro-interaction (e.g., 'the typed-search dropdown', 'the pinned-favorites row') with a one-sentence design rationale that does not reference cross-cultural research, side newsletters, or remote-work context
